key_insight: The paper extends compatible-strain mixed finite elements from simplicial meshes to general quadrilateral meshes for 2D nonlinear elasticity, while preserving the differential-structure-based compatibility of displacement, gradient, and stress fields.
engineering_relevance: This enables more flexible and efficient quadrilateral discretizations for compressible and incompressible solids, often matching the capability of higher-order simplicial CSMFEs with fewer degrees of freedom.
method: Computes compatible shape functions directly in physical space via numerical integration instead of relying on Piola mapping.
Uses first-kind Nedelec elements to interpolate the displacement gradient on quadrilateral elements.
Introduces stress shape functions compatible with the displacement and gradient discretizations; incompressible case uses element-level pressure condensation.
evidence_signal: The abstract reports numerical examples showing the new quadrilateral elements can solve problems that previously required second-order simplicial CSMFEs while using fewer degrees of freedom.
watch_item: Direct physical-space construction avoids Piola limitations but may add implementation complexity and depend on accurate numerical integration for general quadrilateral geometries.
paper_title: First-Order Compatible-Strain Mixed Quadrilateral Finite Elements for 2D Nonlinear Elasticity
